text:
I'd like to try planting (yes, like a plant that'll grow) the next virtual meetup at a different time, so that we can shake things up and bring out some folks who normally can't attend.

If we were to schedule it at 01:00 UTC Aug 27th (conversions: <https://everytimezone.com/?t=68ae4a80,5dc|everytimezone>, <https://time.is/0100_27_August_2025_in_UTC?FoC_Virtual_Meetup|time.is>) — so think evening of wednesday the 26th for north america, morning of the 26th for eastern asia — would you be stoked to attend? to present? give a :+1: on the post if you'd come, and if you might present, post a threaded reply!

text: Works for me, but then I've missed a couple because of life even though the time works in principle. So don't weight me much..

text:
Having done some translating long ago, I can answer that question: it depends on context. You need to figure out what the role of "English" is in this context, and then pick one out of mainly three options:
1. Keep "English"
2. Replace by "Chinese"
3. Reformulate the surrounding paragraph to convey the intended message in a different way.

elements.text: Having done some translating long ago, I can answer that question: it depends on context. You need to figure out what the role of "English" is in this context, and then pick one out of mainly three options:
Keep "English"
Replace by "Chinese"
Reformulate the surrounding paragraph to convey the intended message in a different way.

text:
For sure - the point about the sentence is recognizing that its self-referential, and how that creates a new meaning not purely conveyed by a direct translation of the words. I thought that was interesting relating to:

> Here is the crux of the argument: Translating programs from one language to another, changes their correctness when self-references is involved.

text: Happy GPT-5 day to those who celebrate. I got to play with it at work a bit, and it is impressive. I think having the AI generate symbolic representations of facts in text is going to be a lot more feasible going forward, which is going to be a game changer for people who can figure out how to use it.

text: <@UC2A2ARPT> probably right! Let me know if you need a company to present and I'll happily show what I'm building. If you have a good set of presentations already, I'll do the edition after :)

text: The crux of a universal computer is that you can write an interpreter for any programming language in any Turing Complete programming language. The halting problem is just a mathematical truth, and it is equivalent to Gödel’s Incompleteness Theorem.

ö
del’s Incompleteness Theorem.
